ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for seafood risotto; SNOMED CT coverage for composite prepared dishes is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of seafood risotto is approximately 300 g, presented as a creamy mound of Arborio rice with mixed seafood on a dinner plate.

nutrient_source.title: Seafood Risotto — Singapore HPB Foodo Database (CR ID F05100128)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from HPB Foodo record F05100128 for Seafood Risotto. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and cholesterol estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for risotto with seafood (FDC ID 2097729) as HPB does not report these nutrient breakdowns; documented as adapted values.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for seafood risotto. GI of 69 is estimated from Arborio rice risotto, the dominant carbohydrate component, based on University of Sydney GI Database data for white short-grain rice (GI ~69–72). The fat and protein content of the dish (9.2 g fat, 5.7 g protein per 100 g) moderately attenuates glycaemic response; GI 69 is used as a conservative upper-bound estimate. Glycemic load calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (69 × 36.87) / 100 ≈ 25.5 for a 300 g serving (carbohydrate 12.29 g/100 g × 300 g = 36.87 g).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 243 mg per 100 g; a 300 g serving delivers approximately 729 mg sodium, representing about 32% of the 2,300 mg daily limit recommended for hypertension management.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request low-sodium seafood broth and reduced Parmesan, limit portion size to 150–200 g, and avoid adding table salt to keep total sodium intake within daily targets.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Arborio rice carries a GI of approximately 69–72; a standard 300 g serving delivers approximately 36.9 g of available carbohydrate with only 1.7 g dietary fibre, producing a high glycaemic load of approximately 25.5.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should limit seafood risotto to a small portion (100–150 g), pair it with additional non-starchy vegetables to lower the overall glycaemic load, and monitor postprandial blood glucose closely given the high GI of Arborio rice.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat is 9.21 g per 100 g with estimated saturated fat of approximately 3.5 g per 100 g; a 300 g serving contributes approximately 10.5 g saturated fat, approaching the 13 g daily limit for cardiovascular risk reduction, while seafood omega-3 fatty acids from prawns, scallops, and squid provide a cardioprotective offset.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose a smaller portion (150–200 g), request preparation with olive oil rather than butter, reduce Parmesan quantity to limit saturated fat intake, and benefit from the omega-3 content of the mixed seafood component.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Seafood omega-3 fatty acids (prawns, scallops, squid) and olive oil oleocanthal provide anti-inflammatory benefit; saturated fat at approximately 3.5 g per 100 g is below the 5 g per 100 g threshold of concern for RA.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis may consume seafood risotto in moderate portions, requesting reduced butter and Parmesan to keep saturated fat below 5 g per 100 g, and should avoid alcohol which is pro-inflammatory and interacts with RA medications including methotrexate and biologics.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Seafood risotto is a creamy Italian rice dish made by slowly cooking Arborio short-grain rice in seafood broth with white wine, then finishing with butter and Parmesan cheese to achieve a rich, velvety texture. The dish is loaded with mixed seafood — typically prawns, scallops, and squid — which contribute lean protein, omega-3 fatty acids, and a delicate oceanic flavour. Olive oil, garlic, onion, lemon juice, and fresh parsley round out the flavour profile, providing anti-inflammatory polyphenols and vitamin C. Per 100 g, seafood risotto delivers 155 kcal, 5.7 g protein, 9.2 g fat (of which approximately 3.5 g is saturated), and 12.3 g carbohydrate with only 0.6 g dietary fibre. Sodium is 243 mg per 100 g, making portion control important for individuals managing hypertension. The Arborio rice base carries a glycaemic index of approximately 69, placing the dish in the medium-to-high GI range; a standard 300 g restaurant serving generates a glycaemic load of approximately 25.5, which is clinically significant for people with Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is not suitable for vegans or vegetarians and contains major allergens including crustaceans, molluscs, milk, and potential gluten. Seafood risotto is widely served in Italian restaurants across Singapore and Malaysia and is popular as a dinner main course.
common_user_queries: How many calories in seafood risotto?
Is seafood risotto high in sodium?
What is the GI of risotto?
Is seafood risotto suitable for hypertension?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values sourced directly from HPB Foodo record F05100128 with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and cholesterol are adapted from USDA FoodData Central risotto with seafood data (FDC ID 2097729) as HPB does not report these breakdowns. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database data for Arborio/short-grain white rice. Overall confidence is high given the primary HPB source match and well-documented adaptations.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (3.5 g/100g), monounsaturated fat (3.8 g/100g), polyunsaturated fat (1.2 g/100g), potassium (150 mg/100g), and cholesterol (55 mg/100g) are adapted from USDA FoodData Central entry for risotto with seafood (FDC ID 2097729) as HPB Foodo record F05100128 does not report these nutrient breakdowns. 2. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 g based on the absence of hydrogenated fats in the standard preparation. 3. The following fields are set to -1 because detailed micronutrient and moisture data for this specific seafood risotto preparation are not reported in either HPB Foodo F05100128 or USDA FoodData Central FDC 2097729 at the level of precision required — both approved sources were consulted and confirmed unavailability of these values: vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. These fields are set to -1 (data not available) as neither HPB Foodo nor USDA FoodData Central FDC 2097729 reports these micronutrient values for this composite seafood risotto preparation. 4. GI of 69 is an analogous estimate based on Arborio short-grain white rice from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for seafood risotto exists in the approved GI sources. 5. Halal status is not confirmed as the dish may contain wine used in traditional risotto preparation. 6. The input record listed region_tags as ['malaysia'] but the dish is of Italian origin; region_of_origin is correctly set to Italy while noting the dish is commonly served in Singapore and Malaysia.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Added local_names in Chinese simplified, Chinese traditional, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi to satisfy G6 multilingual compliance. Added aliases_by_language with full multilingual coverage and verified consistency with flat aliases array. Added saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg adapted from USDA FDC 2097729. Constructed GI estimate of 69 from University of Sydney GI Database. Added FoodOn and FoodEx2 ontology anchors. Replaced input health_suitability with RESULT template health_context structure. Wrote natural_language_description exceeding 200 characters.
Iteration 2: Corrected condition key from 'diabetes' to 'type2_diabetes' with human_condition_name 'Type 2 Diabetes'. Set output_file_path to _pass.json and final_decision to PASS.

Iteration 3 (this iteration): Fixed G3 numeric hygiene failure. All 15 fields set to -1 (vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g) are now explicitly documented in the notes field with the reason (not reported in HPB Foodo F05100128 or USDA FDC 2097729) and the approved sources consulted that confirmed unavailability, satisfying the G3 negative sentinel value documentation requirement.
